author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm LXXII

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For Salomon” indeed this Psalm’s title is fore-noted: but things are spoken of therein which could not apply to that Salomon king of Israel after the flesh, according to those things which holy Scripture speaketh concerning him: but they can most pertinently apply to the Lord Christ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence it is perceived, that the very word Salomon is used in a figurative sense, so that in him Christ is to be taken.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For Salomon is interpreted peace-maker: and on this account such a word to Him most truly and excellently doth apply, through Whom, the Mediator, having received remission of sins, we that were enemies are reconciled to God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “when we were enemies we were reconciled to God through the death of His Son.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Same is Himself that Peace-maker.…Since then we have found out the true Salomon, that is, the true Peace-maker: next let us observe what the Psalm doth teach concerning Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “O God, Thy judgment to the King give Thou, and Thy justice to the King’s Son” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Lord Himself in the Gospel saith, “The Father judgeth not any one, but all judgment He hath given to the Son:” this is then, “O God, Thy judgment to the King give Thou.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He that is King is also the Son of the King: because God the Father also is certainly King.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus it hath been written, that the King made a marriage for His Son.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But after the manner of Scripture the same thing is repeated.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For that which he hath said in, “Thy judgment;” the same he hath otherwise expressed in, “Thy justice:” and that which he hath said in, “the King,” the same he hath otherwise expressed in, “to the King’s Son.”…But these repetitions do much commend the divine sayings, whether the same words, or whether in other words the same sense be repeated: and they are mostly found in the Psalms, and in the kind of discourse whereby the mind’s affection is to be awakened.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Next there followeth, “To judge Thy people in justice, and Thy poor in judgment” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what purpose the royal Father gave to the royal Son His judgment and His justice is sufficiently shown when he saith, “To judge Thy people in justice;” that is, for the purpose of judging Thy people.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Such an idiom is found in Salomon: “The Proverbs of Salomon, son of David, to know wisdom and discipline:” that is, the Proverbs of Salomon, for the purpose of knowing wisdom and discipline.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So, “Thy judgment give Thou, to judge Thy people:” that is, “Thy judgment” give Thou for the purpose of judging Thy people.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But that which he saith before in, “Thy people,” the same he saith afterwards in, “Thy poor:” and that which he saith before in, “in justice;” the same afterward in, “in judgment:” according to that manner of repetition.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whereby indeed he showeth, that the people of God ought to be poor, that is, not proud, but humble.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For, “blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In which poverty even blessed Job was poor even before he had lost those great earthly riches.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which thing for this reason I thought should be mentioned, because there are certain persons who are more ready to distribute all their goods to the poor, than themselves to become the poor of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they are puffed up with boasting wherein they think their living well should be ascribed to themselves, not to the grace of God: and therefore now they do not even live well, however great the good works which they seem to do.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But seeing that he hath changed the order of the words (though he had first said, “O God, Thy judgment to the King give Thou, and Thy justice to the King’s Son,” putting judgment first, then justice), and hath put justice first, then judgment, saying, “To judge Thy people in justice, and Thy poor in judgment:” he doth more clearly show that he hath called judgment justice, proving that there is no difference made by the order in which the word is placed, because it signifieth the same thing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is usual to say “wrong judgment” of that which is unjust: but justice iniquitous or unjust we are not wont to speak of.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if wrong and unjust it be; no longer must it be called justice.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Again, by putting down judgment and repeating it under the name of justice, or by putting down justice and repeating it under the name of judgment, he clearly showeth that he specially nameth that judgment which is wont to be put instead of justice, that is, that which cannot be understood of giving an evil judgment.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in the place where He saith, “Judge not according to persons, but right judgment judge ye;” He showeth that there may be a wrong judgment, when He saith, “right judgment judge ye:” lastly, the one He doth forbid, the other He doth enjoin.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when without any addition He speaketh of judgment, He would at once have just judgment to be understood: as is that which He saith, “Ye forsake the weightier matters of the Law, mercy and judgment.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That also which Jeremiah saith is, “making his riches not with judgment.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He saith not, making his riches by wrong or unjust judgment, or not with judgment right or just, but not with judgment: calling not anything judgment but what is right and just.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let the mountains bear peace to the people, and the hills justice” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The mountains are the greater, the hills the less.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These are without doubt those which another Psalm hath, “little with great.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For those mountains did exult like rams, and those hills like lambs of the sheep, at the departure of Israel out of Egypt, that is, at the deliverance of the people of God from this world’s servitude.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Those then that are eminent in the Church for passing sanctity, are the mountains, who are meet to teach other men also, by so speaking as that they may be faithfully taught, by so living as that they may imitate them to their profit: but the hills are they that follow the excellence of the former by their own obedience.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Why then “the mountains peace: and the hills justice”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Would there perchance have been no difference, even if it had been said thus, Let the mountains bear justice to the people and the hills peace?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For to both justice, and to both peace is necessary: and it may be that under another name justice herself may have been called peace.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this is true peace, not such as unjust men make among them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or rather with a distinction not to be overlooked must that be understood which he saith, “the mountains peace, and the hills justice”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For men excelling in the Church ought to counsel for peace with watchful care; lest for the sake of their own distinctions by acting proudly they make schisms and dissever the bond of union.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But let the hills so follow them by imitation and obedience, that they prefer Christ to them: lest being led astray by the empty authority of evil mountains (for they seem to excel), they tear themselves away from the Unity of Christ.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus also most pertinently may be understood, “let the mountains bear peace to the people,” namely, that we understand the peace to consist in the reconciliation whereby we are reconciled to God: for the mountains receive this for His people.…“Let the mountains, therefore, receive peace for the people, and the hills justice:” so that in this manner, both being at one, there may come to pass that which hath been written, “justice and peace have kissed one another.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But that which other copies have, “let the mountains receive peace for the people, and let the hills:” I think must be understood of all sorts of preaching of Gospel peace, whether those that go before, or those that follow after.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But in these copies this followeth, “in justice He shall judge the poor of the people.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But those copies are more approved of which have that which we have expounded above, “let the mountains bear peace to the people, and the hills justice.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But some have, “to Thy people;” some have not to “Thy,” but only “to the people.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He shall judge the poor of the people, and shall save the sons of the poor” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The poor and the sons of the poor seem to me to be the very same, as the same city is Sion and the daughter of Sion.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if it is to be understood with a distinction, the poor we take to be the mountains, but the sons of the poor the hills: for instance, Prophets and Apostles, the poor, but the sons of them, that is, those that profit under their authority, the sons of the poor.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But that which hath been said above, “shall judge;” and afterwards, “shall save;” is as it were a sort of exposition in what manner He shall judge.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For to this end He shall judge, that He may save, that is, may sever from those that are to be destroyed and condemned, those to whom He giveth “salvation ready to be revealed at the” last time.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For by such men to Him is said, “Destroy not with ungodly men my soul:” and, “Judge Thou me, O God, and sever my cause from the nation unholy.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We must observe also that he saith not, He shall judge the poor people, but, “the poor of the people.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For above when he had said, “to judge Thy people in justice and Thy poor in judgment,” the same he called the people of God as His poor, that is, only the good and those that belong to the right hand side.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But because in this world those for the right and those for the left feed together, who, like lambs and goats at the last are to be put asunder; the whole, as it is mingled together, he hath called by the name of the People.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And because even here he putteth judgment in a good sense, that is, for the purpose of saving: therefore he saith, “He shall judge the poor of the people,” that is, shall sever for salvation those that are poor among the people.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He shall humble the false-accuser.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: No false-accuser can be more suitably recognised here than the devil.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: False accusation is his business.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Doth Job worship God gratis?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the Lord Jesus doth humble him, by His grace aiding His own, in order that they may worship God gratis, that is, may take delight in the Lord.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He humbled him also thus; because when in Him the devil, that is, the prince of this world, had found nothing, he slew Him by the false accusations of the Jews, whom the false-accuser made use of as his vessels, working in the sons of unbelief.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He shall endure to the sun,” or, “shall endure with the sun” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For thus some of our writers have thought would be more exactly translated that which in the Greek is συμπαραμενεῖ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if in Latin it could have been expressed in one word, it must have been expressed by compermanebit: however, because in Latin the word cannot be expressed, in order that the sense at least might be translated, it hath been expressed by, “He shall endure with the sun.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For He shall co-endure to the sun is nothing else but, “He shall endure with the sun.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what great matter is it for Him to endure with the sun, through whom all things were made, and without whom nothing was made, save that this prophecy hath been sent before for the sake of those who think that the religion of the Christian name up to a particular time in this world will live, and afterwards will be no more?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He shall endure” therefore “with the sun,” so long as the sun riseth and setteth, that is, so long as these times revolve, there shall not be wanting the Church of God, that is, Christ’s body on earth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But that which he addeth, “and before the moon, generations of generations:” he might have expressed by, and before the sun, that is, both with the sun and before the sun: which would have been understood by both with times and before times.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That then which goeth before time is eternal: and that is truly to be held eternal which by no time is changed, as, “in the beginning was the Word.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But by the moon he hath chosen rather to intimate the waxings and wanings of things mortal.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Lastly, when he had said, “before the moon,” wishing in a manner to explain for what purpose he inserted the moon, “generations,” he saith, “of generations.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As though he were saying, before the moon, that is, before the generations of generations which pass away in the departure and succession of things mortal, like the lunar wanings and waxings.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And thus what is better to be understood by His enduring before the moon, than that He taketh precedence of all mortal things by immortality?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which also as followeth may not impertinently be taken, that whereas now, having humbled the false-accuser, He sitteth at the right hand of the Father, this is to endure with the sun.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the brightness of the eternal glory is understood to be the Son: as though the Sun were the Father, and the Brightness of Him His Son.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But as these things may be spoken of the invisible Substance of the Creator, not as of that visible creation wherein are bodies celestial, of which bright bodies the sun hath the pre-eminence, from which this similitude hath been drawn: just as they are drawn even from things earthly, to wit, stone, lion, lamb, man having two sons, and the like: therefore having humbled the false-accuser, He endureth with the sun: because having vanquished the devil by the Resurrection, He sitteth at the right hand of the Father, where He dieth no more, and death no longer over Him shall have dominion.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This too is before the moon, as though the First-born from the dead were going before the Church, which is passing on in the departure and succession of mortals.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These are “the generations of generations.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or perchance it is because generations are those whereby we are begotten mortally; but generations of generations those whereby we are begotten again immortally.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And such is the Church which He went before, in order that He might endure before the moon, being the First-born of the dead.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To be sure, that which is in the Greek γενεας γενεῶν, some have interpreted, not “generations,” but, “of a generation of generations:” because γενεας is of ambiguous case in Greek, and whether it be the genitive singular της γενε‚ς, that is, of the generation, or the accusative plural τὰς γενεὰς, that is, the generations, doth not clearly appear, except that deservedly that sense hath been preferred wherein, as though explaining what he had called “the moon,” he added in continuation, “generations of generations.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He shall come down like rain into a fleece, and like drops distilling upon the earth” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He hath called to our minds and admonished us, that what was done by Gedeon the Judge, in Christ hath its end.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he asked a sign of the Lord, that a fleece laid on the floor should alone be rained upon, and the floor should be dry; and again, the fleece alone should be dry, and the floor should be rained upon; and so it came to pass.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which thing signified, that, being as it were on a floor in the midst of the whole round world, the dry fleece was the former people Israel.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The same Christ therefore Himself came down like rain upon a fleece, when yet the floor was dry: whence also He said, “I am not sent but to the sheep which were lost of the house of Israel.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There He chose out a Mother by whom to receive the form of a servant, wherein He was to appear to men: there the disciples, to whom He gave this same injunction, saying, “Into the way of the nations go ye not away, and into the cities of the Samaritans enter ye not: go ye first to the sheep which are lost of the house of Israel.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When He saith, go ye first to them, He showeth also that hereafter, when at length the floor was to be rained upon, they would go to other sheep also, which were not of the old people Israel, concerning whom He saith, “I have other sheep which are not of this fold, it behoveth Me to bring in them also, that there may be one flock and one Shepherd.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence also the Apostle: “for I say,” he saith, “that Christ was a minister of the Circumcision for the truth of God, to confirm the promises of the fathers.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus rain came down upon the fleece, the floor being yet dry.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But inasmuch as he continueth, “but that the nations should glorify God for His mercy:” that when the time came on, that should be fulfilled which by the Prophet He saith, “a people whom I have not known hath served Me, in the hearkening of the ear it hath obeyed Me:” we now see, that of the grace of Christ the nation of the Jews hath remained dry, and the whole round world through all nations is being rained upon by clouds full of Christian grace.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For by another word he hath indicated the same rain, saying, “drops distilling:” no longer upon the fleece, but “upon the earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what else is rain but drops distilling?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But that the above nation under the name of a fleece is signified, I think is either because they were to be stripped of the authority of teaching, just as a sheep is stripped of its skin; or because in a secret place He was hiding that same rain, which He willed not should be preached to uncircumcision, that is, be revealed to uncircumcised nations.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “There shall arise in His days justice and abundance of peace, until the moon be taken away” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The expression tollatur some have interpreted by “be taken away,” but others by “be exalted,” translating one Greek word, which is there used, ‡νταναιρεθῇ, just as each of them thought good.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But they who have said, “be removed,” and they who have said, “be taken away,” do not so very much differ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For by the expression, “be removed,” custom doth teach us that there should be rather implied, that a thing is taken away and is no more, than that it is raised to a higher place: but “be taken away” can be understood in no other way at all, than that a thing is destroyed: that is, it is no more: but by “be exalted,” only that it is raised to a higher place.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which indeed when it is put in a bad sense is wont to signify pride: as is the passage, “In thy wisdom be not exalted.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But in a good sense it belongeth to a more exceeding honour, as, for instance, when anything is being raised; as is, “In the nights exalt ye your hands unto holy places, and bless ye the Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here then if we have understood the expression, “be removed,” what will be, “until the moon be removed,” but that it be so dealt with that it be no more?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For perchance he willed this also to be perceived, that mortality is to be no longer, “when the last enemy shall be destroyed, death:” so that abundance of peace may be brought down so far as that nothing may withstand the felicity of the blessed from the infirmity of mortality: which will come to pass in that age, of which we have the faithful promise of God through Jesus Christ our Lord, concerning which it is said, “There shall arise in His days justice and abundance of peace:” until, death being utterly overcome and destroyed, all mortality be consumed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if under the term moon, not the mortality of the flesh through which the Church is now passing, but the Church Herself in general hath been signified, which is to endure for everlasting, being delivered from this mortality, thus must be taken the expression, “There shall arise in His days justice and abundance of peace, until the moon be exalted;” as though it were said, There shall arise in His days justice, to conquer the contradiction and rebellion of the flesh, and whereby there may be made a peace so increasing and abundant, until the moon be exalted, that is, until the Church be lifted up, through the glory of the Resurrection to reign with Him, who went before Her in this glory, the first-born of the dead, that He might sit at the right hand of the Father; thus with the sun enduring before the moon, in the place whereunto hereafter was to be exalted the moon also.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He shall be Lord from sea even unto sea, and from the river even unto the ends of the round world” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 8): He to wit concerning whom he had said, “There shall arise in His days justice and abundance of peace, until the moon be exalted.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If the Church here is properly signified under the term moon, in continuation he showed how widely that same Church He was going to spread abroad, when He added, “and He shall be Lord from sea even unto sea.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the land is encircled by a great sea which is called the Ocean: from which there floweth in some small part in the midst of the lands, and maketh those seas known to us, which are frequented by ships.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Again, in “from sea even unto sea” He hath said, that from any one end of the earth even unto any other end, He would be Lord, whose name and power in the whole world were to be preached and to prevail exceedingly.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To which, that there might not be understood in any other manner, “from sea even unto sea:” He immediately added, “and from the river even unto the ends of the round world.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore that which He saith in “even unto the ends of the round world,” the same He had said before in “from sea even unto sea.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But in that which now He saith, “from the river,” He hath evidently expressed that He willed Christ to publish at length His power from that place from whence also He began to choose His disciples, to wit from the river Jordan, where upon the Lord, on His baptism, when the Holy Ghost descended, there sounded a voice from Heaven, “This is My beloved Son.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From this place then His doctrine and the authority of the heavenly ministry setting out, is enlarged even unto the ends of the round world, when there is preached the Gospel of the kingdom in the whole world, for a testimony unto all nations: and then shall come the end.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “In His presence shall fall down the Ethiopians, and His enemies shall lick the earth” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By the Ethiopians, as by a part the whole, He hath signified all nations, selecting that nation to mention especially by name, which is at the ends of the earth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By “in His presence shall fall down” hath been signified, shall adore Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And because there were to be schisms in divers quarters of the world, which would be jealous of the Church Catholic spread abroad in the whole round world, and again those same schisms dividing themselves into the names of men, and by loving the men under whose authority they had been rent, opposing themselves to the glory of Christ which is throughout all lands; so when He had said, “in His presence shall fall down the Ethiopians,” He added, “and His enemies shall lick the earth:” that is, shall love men, so that they shall be jealous of the glory of Christ, to whom hath been said, “Be Thou exalted above the Heavens, O God, and above all the earth Thy glory.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For man earned to hear, “Earth thou art, and unto earth thou shalt go.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By licking this earth, that is, being delighted with the vainly talking authority of such men, by loving them, and by counting them for the most pleasing of men, they gainsay the divine sayings, whereby the Catholic Church hath been foretold, not as to be in any particular quarter of the world, as certain schisms are, but in the whole universe by bearing fruit and growing so as to attain even unto the very Ethiopians, to wit, the remotest and foulest of mankind.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The kings of Tharsis and the isles shall offer gifts, the kings of the Arabians and of Saba shall lead presents” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This no longer requireth an expounder but a thinker; yea it doth thrust itself upon the sight not only of rejoicing believers, but also of groaning unbelievers—except perchance we must inquire why there hath been said, “shall lead presents.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there are wont to be led those things which can walk.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For could it by any means have been spoken with reference to the sacrifice of victims?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Far be it that such “righteousness” should arise in His days.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But those gifts which have been foretold as to be led, seem to me to signify men, whom into the fellowship of the Church of Christ the authority of kings doth lead: although even persecuting kings have led gifts, knowing not what they did, in sacrificing the holy Martyrs.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And there shall adore Him all kings of the earth, all nations shall serve Him” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But while he is explaining the reasons why so great honour is paid Him by kings, and He is served of all nations: “because He hath delivered,” he saith, “the needy man from the mighty, and the poor man, to whom was no helper”(ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This needy and poor man is the people of men believing in Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In this people are also kings adoring Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they do not disdain to be needy and poor, that is, humbly confessing sins, and needing the glory of God and the grace of God, in order that this King, Son of the King, may deliver them from the mighty one.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this same mighty one is he who above was called the Slanderer: whom mighty to subdue men to himself, and to hold them bound in captivity, not his virtue did make, but men’s sins.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The same is himself also called strong; therefore here mighty also.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But He that hath humbled the slanderer and hath entered into the house of the strong man to bind him and to spoil his vessels, He “hath delivered the needy and the poor man.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this neither the virtue of any one could accomplish, nor any just man, nor any Angel.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When then there was no helper, by His coming He saved them Himself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it might occur to one; if because of sins man was held by the devil, have sins pleased Christ, who saved the needy man from the mighty?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But “He it is that shall spare the helpless and poor man” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 13): that is, shall remit sins to the man, humble and not trusting in his own merits, or hoping for salvation because of his own virtue, but needing the grace of his Saviour.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when he hath added, “and the souls of the poor He shall save:” he hath recommended to our notice both the aids of grace; both that which is for the remission of sins, when he saith, “He shall spare the poor and needy man;” and that which doth consist in the imparting of righteousness, when he hath added, “and the souls of the poor He shall save.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For no one is meet of himself for salvation (which salvation is perfect righteousness), unless God’s grace aid: because the fulness of the law is nought but love, which doth not exist in us of ourselves, but is shed abroad in our hearts through the Holy Spirit which hath been given unto us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “From usuries and iniquity He shall redeem the souls of them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What are these usuries but sins, which are also called debts?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But I think they have been called usuries, because more of ill is found in the punishments than hath been committed in the sins.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For, for example’s sake, while a man-slayer killeth only the body of a man, but can no wise hurt the soul; of himself both soul and body is destroyed in hell.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Because of such despisers of present commandment and deriders of future punishment hath been said, “I coming would have exacted with usuries,” from these usuries are redeemed the souls of the poor by that blood which hath been shed for the remission of sins.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He shall redeem, I say, from usuries, by remitting sins which owed larger punishments: but He shall redeem from iniquity, by helping them by grace even to do righteousness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore the same two things have been repeated which were said above.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in that which is above, “He shall spare the helpless and poor man,” there is understood “from usuries:” but in that which there he saith, “and the souls of the poor He shall save;” there seemeth to have been implied, “from iniquity:” so that the words “He shall redeem,” are understood with both.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So when He shall spare the poor and helpless man, and shall save the souls of the poor: thus “from usuries and iniquity He shall redeem the souls of them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And honourable shall be the name of Him in the presence of them.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they give honour to His name for so great benefits, and they respond that “meet and right it is” to render thanks to the Lord their God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or, as some copies have it, “and honourable is the name of them in the presence of Him:” for even if Christians seem despicable to this world, the name of them in the presence of Him is honourable, who to them hath given it, no longer remembering those names in His lips, whereby before they used to be called, when they were bound fast by the superstitions of the Gentiles, or signed with names derived from their own evil deserts, before they were Christians, which name is honourable in the presence of Him, even if it seemeth despicable to enemies.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He shall live, and there shall be given to Him of the gold of Arabia” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There would not have been said, “and He shall live” (for of whom could not this be said, though living for ever so brief a space of time on this earth?) unless that life were being recommended to our notice, wherein He “dieth no more, and death over Him shall have no more dominion.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And thus, “and He shall live,” that was despised in death: for, as another Prophet saith, “there shall be taken away from the earth the life of Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what is, “and there shall be given to Him of the gold of Arabia”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the fact that from thence even the former Salomon received gold, in this Psalm hath been in a figure transferred unto another true Salomon, that is, the true Peace-maker.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the former did not have dominion “from the river even unto the ends of the round world.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus then hath been prophesied, that even the wise men of this world in Christ would believe.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But by Arabia we understand the Gentiles; by gold wisdom which doth as much excel among all doctrines as gold among metals.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence hath been written, “Receive ye prudence as silver, and wisdom as proved gold.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And they shall pray concerning Himself alway.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That which the Greek hath, περὶ αὐτοῦ, some have interpreted by “concerning Himself,” some “for Himself,” or “for Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what is, “concerning Himself,” except perchance that for which we pray, saying, “Thy kingdom come”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For Christ’s coming shall make present to believers the kingdom of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But how to understand “for Him” is difficult; except that when prayer is made for the Church, for Himself prayer is made, because she is His Body.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For concerning Christ and the Church hath been sent before a great Sacrament, “there shall be two in one flesh.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But now that which followeth, “all the day long,” that is, in all time, “they shall bless Him,” is sufficiently evident.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And there shall be a firmament on the earth, on the tops of the mountains” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For, “all the promises of God in Him are Yea,” that is, in Him are confirmed: because in Him hath been fulfilled whatever hath been prophesied for our salvation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the tops of the mountains it is meet to understand as the authors of the divine Scriptures, that is, those persons through whom they were supplied: wherein He is indeed Himself the Firmament: for unto Him all things that have been divinely written are ascribed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But this He willed should be on earth; because for the sake of those that are upon earth, they were written.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence He came also Himself upon earth, in order that He might confirm all these things, that is, in Himself might show them to have been fulfilled.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For it was necessary,” He saith, “for all things to be fulfilled which were written in the Law, and the Prophets, and Psalms, concerning Me:” that is, “in the tops of the mountain.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For so there cometh in the last time the evident Mount of the Lord, prepared on the summit of the mountains: of which here he speaketh, “in the tops of the mountains.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Highly superexalted above Libanus shall be His fruit.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Libanus we are wont to take as this world’s dignity: for Libanus is a mountain bearing tall trees, and the name itself is interpreted whiteness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what marvel, if above every brilliant state of this world there is superexalted the fruit of Christ, of which fruit the lovers have contemned all secular dignities?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if in a good sense we take Libanus, because of the “cedars of Libanus which He hath planted:” what other fruit must be understood, that is being exalted above this Libanus, except that whereof the Apostle speaketh when he is going to speak concerning that love of his, “yet a pre-eminent way to you I show”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this is put forward even in the first rank of divine gifts, in the place where he saith, “but the fruit of the Spirit is love:” and with this are conjoined the remaining words as consequent.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And they shall flourish from the city like hay of the earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Because city is used ambiguously, and there is not annexed of Him, or of God, for there hath not been said, “from the city” of Him, or “from the city” of God, but only “from the city:” in a good sense it is understood, in order that from the city of God, that is, from the Church, they may flourish like grass; but grass bearing fruit, as is that of wheat: for even this is called grass in Holy Scripture; as in Genesis there is a command for the earth to bring forth every tree and every grass, and there is not added every wheat: which without doubt would not have been passed over unless under the name of grass this also were understood; and in many other passages of the Scriptures this is found.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if we must take, “and they shall flourish like the grass of the earth,” in the same manner as is said, “all flesh is grass, and the glory of a man like the flower of grass:” certainly then that city must be understood which doth intimate this world’s society: for it was not to no purpose that Cain was the first to build a city.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus the fruit of Christ being exalted above Libanus, that is, above enduring trees and undecaying timbers, because He is the everlasting fruit, all the glory of a man according to the temporal exaltation of the world is compared to grass; for by believers and by men already hoping for life eternal temporal felicity is despised, in order that there may be fulfilled that which hath been written, “all flesh is grass, and all the glory of flesh as the flower of grass: the grass hath dried, the flower hath fallen off, but the word of the Lord doth endure for ever.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is the fruit of Him exalted above Libanus.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For always flesh hath been grass, and the glory of flesh as the flower of grass: but because it was not clearly proved what felicity ought to have been chosen and preferred, the flower of grass was esteemed for a great matter: not only it was by no means despised, but it was even chiefly sought after.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As if therefore at that time He shall have begun to be thus, when there is reproved and despised whatever used to flourish in the world, thus hath been said, “superexalted above Libanus shall be the fruit of Him, and they shall flourish from the city like grass of the earth:” that is, glorified above all things shall be that which is promised for everlasting, and compared to the grass of the earth shall be whatever is counted a great matter in the world.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Be,” therefore, “the name of Him blessed for ever: before the sun endureth the name of Him” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By the sun times are signified.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore for everlasting endureth the name of Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For eternity doth precede times, and is not bounded by time.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And there shall be blessed in Him all the tribes of the earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in Him is fulfilled that which hath been promised to Abraham.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For He saith not, In seeds, as though in many; but as though in one, And to thy Seed, which is Christ.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But to Abraham is said, “In thy Seed shall be blessed all the tribes of the earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And not the sons of the flesh but the sons of promise are counted in the Seed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “All nations shall magnify Him.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As if in explanation there is repeated that which above hath been said.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For because they shall be blessed in Him, they shall magnify Him; not of themselves making Him to be great, that of Himself is great, but by praising and confessing Him to be great.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For thus we magnify God: thus also we say, “Hallowed be Thy name,” which is indeed always holy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, who hath done wonderful things alone” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Contemplating all things above spoken of, a hymn bursteth forth; and the Lord God of Israel is blessed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For that is being fulfilled which hath been spoken to that barren woman, “and He that hath delivered Thee, the God of Israel, shall Himself be called of the whole earth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He doeth” Himself “marvellous things alone:” for whosoever do them, He doth Himself work in them, “who doeth wonderful things alone.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And blessed be the name of His glory for everlasting, and for age of age” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what else should the Latin interpreters have said, who could not have said for everlasting, and for everlasting of everlasting?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it soundeth as if one thing were meant in the expression “for everlasting,” and another thing in the expression “for age:” but the Greek hath εἰς τὸν αἰωνα, καὶ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα τοῦ αἰῶνος, which perchance more meetly might have been rendered by, “for age, and for age of age:” so that by “for age,” might have been understood as long as this age endureth; but “for age of age,” that which after the end of this is promised to be.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And there shall be fulfilled with the glory of Him every land: so be it, so be it.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou hast commanded, O Lord, so it is coming to pass: so it is coming to pass, until that which began with the river, may attain fully even unto the ends of the round world.
